title: Pull-sync: fetch workspace/member/project config from fleet-dashboard into local JSON cache
description:
The core 'reliable storage + easy bootstrap' value-add the product owner asked for. When apra-fleet.exe is SaaS-connected, fetch the caller's workspace(s), and within a selected workspace its members/projects, from fleet-dashboard's real API (GET /v1/workspaces, GET /v1/ws/:id/members, GET /v1/ws/:id/projects per fleet-dashboard/docs/api-contract.md), and cache the result as local JSON extending src/services/registry.ts's existing pattern -- explicitly NOT a local relational database, per the product-owner directive.

Depends on apra-fleet-48p (design) for: what gets cached and where, sync trigger (automatic vs explicit), and conflict/staleness handling.
acceptance_criteria: A SaaS-connected apra-fleet.exe instance can pull its workspace's member/project list from fleet-dashboard and have it reflected in local commands (e.g. list_members) without requiring any local Postgres/SQL. Standalone (non-SaaS) operation is provably unaffected -- existing local-only tests pass unmodified. Tests use fleet-dashboard's real documented response shapes, not hub-service's retiring shapes.
notes: Cross-repo prerequisite (per AGREED docs/bootstrap-sync-design-proposal.md): do not start until fleet-dashboard builds GET /v1/ws/:id/bootstrap (filed as fleet-dashboard-1il in their tracker -- does not exist yet). Pull-sync fetches from this endpoint, not from session-only list routes.

title: Repoint apra-fleet join <token> enrollment to fleet-dashboard's real API
description:
src/cli/join.ts today exchanges an enrollment token for a machine JWT against apra-fleet-reorg's OWN hub-service (POST /join/exchange, src/hub-service/enrollment.ts). Per this epic's decision, hub-service is retiring to reference-only -- join.ts needs to speak to fleet-dashboard's real API instead.

Cross-repo dependency: fleet-dashboard's docs/api-contract.md does not currently document an enrollment-token flow (see docs/api-contract-reconciliation.md item 22 in this repo, which recommended fleet-dashboard's contract adopt one). This task's exact shape depends on the design task (apra-fleet-48p) resolving whether to wait for that contract addition or build against fleet-dashboard's existing POST /v1/ws/:id/members (member registration + one-time JWT issuance) as a fallback path.

Scope: update join.ts's HubCredentials/exchange logic to target fleet-dashboard's base URL and real endpoint shape (whichever the design task settles on), with tests using fleet-dashboard's actual documented response shapes (not apra-fleet-reorg's own hub-jwt.ts claims shape, which is being retired).
acceptance_criteria: apra-fleet join <token> successfully exchanges a token for credentials against fleet-dashboard's real API (or a faithful mock of its documented contract), with tests. src/hub-service/enrollment.ts is no longer apra-fleet.exe's enrollment target (may remain as reference code per apra-fleet-yp3).
notes: Cross-repo prerequisite (per AGREED docs/bootstrap-sync-design-proposal.md): do not start until fleet-dashboard-anw (ironWall missing on members.ts, in fleet-dashboard's own tracker) is CLOSED. Building enrollment's 1b flow against POST /v1/ws/:id/members while it has a workspace-isolation hole would mean enrollment inherits that hole.

title: Design: bootstrap/sync contract, sync policy, and enrollment auth flow against fleet-dashboard
description:
Precedes all implementation work in this epic. Design questions that need explicit answers before code is written, since guessing wrong here means redoing downstream work:

1. Sync direction and conflict resolution: is it pull-only (cloud is authoritative, local registry.json is a read-through cache), push-only (local registrations get mirrored up, cloud never overwrites local), or bidirectional reconcile (and if so, what wins on conflict -- last-write-wins by timestamp, cloud-always-wins, or explicit user prompt)?
2. Sync trigger: automatic the moment a device is SaaS-connected (every registry read/write also touches the network), or an explicit opt-in action per workspace/member/project (e.g. 'apra-fleet sync' command, or only new registrations get pushed, existing local-only agents stay local-only until explicitly migrated)?
3. Enrollment/auth: today's 'apra-fleet join <token>' (src/cli/join.ts) exchanges a token for a machine JWT against apra-fleet-reorg's OWN hub-service (POST /join/exchange). Under the new architecture this must point at fleet-dashboard's real API instead. fleet-dashboard's docs/api-contract.md does not currently document an enrollment-token flow at all (see docs/api-contract-reconciliation.md item 22, recommended CONTRACT ADAPTS to add one) -- this is a cross-repo dependency, not something apra-fleet-reorg can finish alone. Design what apra-fleet-reorg's client-side shape should be given the contract's EXISTING member-registration endpoint (POST /v1/ws/:id/members issues a JWT once) as a fallback if the enrollment-token section never lands, vs. waiting for it.
4. What exactly gets cached locally as JSON, and where: extend src/services/registry.ts's existing registry.json shape, or a new sibling file (e.g. cloud-workspace-cache.json)? Must not introduce a local relational DB per the product-owner directive.
5. Failure/offline behavior: if fleet-dashboard is unreachable, does apra-fleet.exe fail closed (block local operations pending sync) or fail open (operate on stale local cache, retry sync later)? Given 'today's standalone mode must keep working,' fail-open matching standalone behavior is the likely answer, but state it explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: A short design doc (docs/) answering all 5 questions above, reviewed before any of this epic's other children start implementation. Explicitly confirms zero impact on standalone (no-cloud) operation as a design constraint, not just an aspiration.
close_reason: AGREED via docs/cross-repo-design-protocol.md's autonomous negotiation loop -- 2 rounds, both spawned as fresh agents scoped to fleet-dashboard's real code, zero human relay needed since both repos are local to this machine. Round 1 found the original plan was unimplementable (member JWTs have zero read access in fleet-dashboard's real API) plus a real security bug in fleet-dashboard's own code (filed as fleet-dashboard-anw). Round 2 confirmed the revised resolution (dedicated GET /v1/ws/:id/bootstrap endpoint; member registration stays human-session-only, permanently) with zero open objections. Full record: docs/bootstrap-sync-design-proposal.md (Status: AGREED) and fleet-dashboard's docs/bootstrap-sync-design-response.md (both rounds).

title: [EPIC] SaaS bootstrap/sync integration (apra-fleet.exe <-> fleet-dashboard) + hub-service retirement
description:
Follow-on to the hub-spoke migration (apra-fleet-us9, closed) and the API contract reconciliation (docs/api-contract-reconciliation.md). Product-owner directive (2026-07-05): fleet-dashboard is the ONLY true persistence layer for workspace/project/member/secret configuration; apra-fleet.exe must never reimplement that persistence, even for a self-hosted story. Cross-checked against fleet-dashboard's own docs/architecture.md, which confirms this as their real, tested architecture (DynamoDB-backed, OAuth+session-cookie auth already built, iron-wall workspace isolation already tested).

User's explicit scoping decision (2026-07-05), reconciling this with today's working product: (1) today's standalone apra-fleet.exe (local registry.json, LocalStrategy, RemoteStrategy/SSH) continues to work exactly as-is, zero regression, SSH is NOT deprecated or gated behind anything -- it remains a permanent first-class execution transport, not a migration target. (2) The cloud/SaaS value-add is specifically: (a) easy bootstrap of workspaces/members/projects via a web UI instead of local-only CLI setup, and (b) reliable, durable storage of that configuration off fragile developer devices -- NOT replacing SSH-based execution with a relay. (3) src/hub-service/ (the Postgres-backed hub built this session) is retired to REFERENCE-ONLY status -- valuable as a working proof of the wire-protocol/security-isolation semantics, but fleet-dashboard is the real production tier-3 implementation going forward; hub-service is not deployed or extended further. (4) Relay/NAT-traversal command execution (the envelope/SSE system) is explicitly DEFERRED -- fleet-dashboard's own bidirectional WebSocket relay is 'designed, not yet built' per their architecture.md, so no urgent need to build or maintain a competing apra-fleet-reorg-side relay right now. Focus is the bootstrap/sync integration first.
acceptance_criteria: apra-fleet.exe gains an opt-in SaaS-connected mode that can bootstrap workspace/member/project config from fleet-dashboard and keep local registry.json durably synced, while standalone (no-cloud) operation is provably unchanged (existing test suite for local/SSH paths continues to pass with zero modification). src/hub-service/ is clearly marked reference-only in its own docs and the project's top-level docs, with no further production feature work landing there.

title: Spoke-side hub-client: outbound connect/reconnect/backoff/presence/heartbeat
description: The apra-fleet.exe (tier 2) side of docs/hub-spoke-wire-protocol.md: a hub-client module that (1) reads hub-credentials.json (written by src/cli/join.ts), (2) opens the outbound SSE stream to the hub with exponential backoff+jitter (base 1s, cap 60s, no backoff reset until a heartbeat ack is actually processed), (3) POSTs presence.announce on connect with the full current member snapshot from the local registry, (4) runs the 15s heartbeat loop honoring the hub's next_heartbeat_due_ms, (5) keeps a short-lived (5 min) seen-envelope_id cache to no-op duplicate at-least-once redeliveries. This is the biggest reversal named in us9.6's own description: apra-fleet.exe becomes a thin relay/auth-passthrough rather than the JWT-minting authority. Does NOT yet include actually executing relayed commands (that is a follow-on task, since it needs to reuse LocalStrategy safely).
acceptance_criteria: A fake-timers/fake-fetch test proves: connect posts presence.announce with the right snapshot; a dropped SSE stream triggers backoff reconnect with the documented schedule; heartbeat loop respects server-supplied next_heartbeat_due_ms; a duplicate envelope_id delivery is a no-op per the seen-cache.
close_reason: Implemented src/services/hub-client.ts (commit 251b0c0): outbound SSE connect/reconnect with exponential backoff+jitter (base 1s/cap 60s, resets only on a processed heartbeat ack per wire-protocol.md), presence.announce with full member snapshot on connect, heartbeat loop honoring server-supplied next_heartbeat_due_ms, and a 5-min seen-envelope_id cache for at-least-once dedup. All I/O injected (fetch/timers/random/clock) for deterministic testing -- 12 new tests covering the pure backoff/cache/SSE-parser logic plus full reconnect/dedup/error-logging orchestration scenarios. Does not execute relayed commands itself (that's apra-fleet-cgg, now unblocked). 2070 tests total, tsc --noEmit clean.

title: Hub-side SSE delivery stream + TTL-expiry failed-envelope generation + queue depth cap
description: Completes the hub-side half of docs/hub-spoke-wire-protocol.md sections 5-6, building on the envelope-admission slice (submitEnvelope/relay-queue.ts) already done in apra-fleet-us9.6. Needed: (1) GET SSE stream endpoint that pushes deliverable envelopes to a connected spoke immediately (not just poll-on-demand), tracking ack_timeout_ms=10s re-delivery; (2) sweepExpired() already exists in relay-queue.ts but nothing yet generates the synthetic <kind>.failed result envelope back to the originator on TTL expiry (section 6); (3) per-member queue depth cap (1000 envelopes / 8 MiB) at admission time in enqueue(), rejecting the newest attempt with an explicit error rather than silently dropping an older item.
acceptance_criteria: SSE stream delivers a queued envelope to a connected test client within the ack timeout; re-delivers on missed ack; TTL-expired envelope produces a synthetic failed result addressed back to the originator; enqueue() rejects the newest admission once a member's queue hits the depth/size cap. All proven with real pg-mem + real HTTP tests, no mocking of the delivery logic.
close_reason: Implemented (commit 2747ca6): GET /ws/:id/stream SSE push route; relay_queue gained origin_member_id + delivered_at (migration 007) so fetchDeliverable() honors ack_timeout_ms=10s redeliver-on-timeout instead of redeliver-on-every-poll; sweepExpiredToFailures() generates a synthetic <result-kind> envelope (payload.status=target_offline_ttl_expired) back to the originator on TTL expiry for request kinds (execute_command/execute_prompt/send_message.deliver), wired as a 5s background sweep in main.ts; enqueue() enforces the 1000-envelope/8MiB per-target queue depth cap, rejecting the newest admission (HTTP 429) rather than evicting an older item. 11 new tests, 2082 total, tsc --noEmit clean. This closes out apra-fleet-us9.6's three scoped child issues (b55/3jg/cgg) -- the spoke-mode wire-protocol plumbing is now implemented hub-admission-to-execution-to-ack end-to-end.

title: execute_prompt allows two concurrent invocations against the same local member, causing repo/git corruption
description:
Real data-loss incident (2026-07-02/03, apra-fleet-reorg checkout): two overlapping execute_prompt invocations ran against the same local member (fleet-reorg) and the same working directory at once, destroying the .git directory (and .gitignore/.gitattributes/.gitmodules/.github/.claude) mid-rebase. Full forensic account in that checkout's RECOVERY.md. Content survived (verified via a tree snapshot + npm test 1772/0 passing minutes before the loss); 3 real local commits' history did not (folded into one squashed recovery commit). Nothing was ever pushed, so no remote/shared state was affected -- but this could just as easily corrupt work that matters more, or happen on a shared/remote member.

Suspected root cause: an execute_prompt call that hits its inactivity timeout (timeout_s) is reported to the caller as failed, but the underlying spawned provider-CLI process on the target machine is not necessarily killed when that happens -- for a LOCAL member this is a real OS process on the same machine as the server. A subsequent dispatch to the same member (especially resume:true, continuing the same session) can then start a second process against the same working directory while the first is still alive and mid-edit. Two process trees mutating one git working tree/index concurrently is exactly the kind of race that can delete/corrupt .git.

This matters more, not less, once the hub-spoke migration (apra-fleet-us9) lands: the hub will relay dispatches to a target machine's apra-fleet.exe, and the same one-session-per-member assumption needs to hold under relay too, or the corruption risk just moves from "orchestrator dispatches too fast" to "hub relays too fast".

Suggested fix directions (not decided, needs investigation):
- Verify/enforce that hitting timeout_s actually kills the underlying process tree (not just stops the server from waiting on it) before execute_prompt returns a failure to the caller.
- Add a per-member (or per-session) lock/mutex in the apra-fleet server so a second execute_prompt dispatch to a member with an already-in-flight session is rejected or queued, never silently run concurrently.
- On resume:true specifically, verify the target session's prior process has actually exited (not just that a previous tool call returned) before spawning a new one.
acceptance_criteria:
- Root cause confirmed with a reproduction (or as close to one as feasible: demonstrate that a timed-out execute_prompt's underlying process can still be alive after the tool reports failure).
- A concurrency guard exists (lock/reject/queue -- whichever is chosen) preventing two execute_prompt invocations from running against the same local member's working directory at once.
- A test covers the guard: dispatching a second execute_prompt to a member with an in-flight session is rejected/queued, not run concurrently.
- Behavior documented for what happens to the caller when a dispatch is rejected/queued for this reason (clear error, not a silent hang or silent double-run).
notes: Model: opus -- concurrency/process-lifecycle bug in core dispatch infrastructure, cross-cutting and high blast-radius (affects every member, and more so once hub relay lands); needs careful investigation, not a mechanical fix.
close_reason: Root-caused and fixed: LocalStrategy's timeout/abort kill only signalled the immediate shell wrapper, not the process tree. Fixed with a synchronous OS-level tree-kill (taskkill /F /T on Windows) run before the redundant child.kill(), reproduced and verified with a real nested-process test (tests/strategy-process-tree-kill.test.ts). Also added a test for the pre-existing inFlightAgents concurrency guard (tests/execute-prompt.test.ts). All 4 acceptance criteria met; full suite green (119 files / 1837 tests).

title: Extract and publish shared fleet-hub API contract (Zod schemas + generated OpenAPI)
description:
Single source of truth for the hub<->dashboard API contract, so apra-fleet's hub-service (us9.4) and fleet-dashboard's real frontend (us9.10) never silently drift from each other -- both currently only share a prose README sketch + a mock-data JS file (cloud/data.jsx in C:\akhil\git\fleet-dashboard).

DECIDED: lives inside apra-fleet as a workspace package (not a dedicated repo). fleet-dashboard consumes it as a published dependency. Rationale from the human: the JWT is the main bridge that lets a member/apra-fleet.exe instance use the service at all -- JWTClaims is therefore the central, load-bearing export of this package, not just one schema among several. Everything else (Workspace, Project, Member, UsageRecord, ActivityEvent, Installer CRUD) is gated behind a valid, workspace-scoped JWT, so the package should treat JWTClaims (and ideally the sign/verify helpers already in src/services/jwt.ts) as the anchor other schemas reference, not a peer schema defined independently.

Define Zod schemas for: Workspace, Project, Member (provider enum must include 'none' per us9.14), JWTClaims {iss, ws, sub, exp, role} (ws = workspace_id, the hard scope per hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 3), UsageRecord (project, member, tokens, cost -- see us9.15), ActivityEvent, Installer. Generate OpenAPI 3.1 from the same schemas (zod-to-openapi or equivalent) covering the endpoint list in fleet-dashboard/README.md "State & API sketch".

Publish as a versioned package (npm private registry or GitHub Packages) from apra-fleet's monorepo; fleet-dashboard's real frontend (once built past the hi-fi prototype) depends on it like any other npm package.

Add contract tests in the hub-service (us9.4) that validate real API responses against these schemas at runtime, not just types -- catches drift that type-checking alone would miss (e.g. an extra/missing field the wire format actually sends).
acceptance_criteria:
- Zod schemas exist for every entity in cloud/data.jsx (Workspace, Project, Member, JWTClaims, UsageRecord, ActivityEvent, Installer) plus request/response shapes for every endpoint in fleet-dashboard/README.md's API sketch.
- JWTClaims is the anchor schema -- every other entity/endpoint schema that requires auth references it explicitly (not redefined ad hoc per endpoint).
- OpenAPI 3.1 spec generated from the same Zod schemas (not hand-written separately -- single source, no dual maintenance).
- Package lives as a workspace package inside apra-fleet, published and versioned (semver); fleet-dashboard has a documented path to consume it as a normal npm dependency.
- At least one contract test in the hub-service validates a real handler's response against the published schema at runtime.
notes: Model: sonnet -- scoped implementation against a well-specified source (cloud/data.jsx + README API sketch already exist); repo-placement decision made by the human (apra-fleet workspace package).
close_reason: Closed

title: Dashboard OAuth + RBAC + admin provisioning (human user auth)
description:
A second, separate auth system from member JWT/workspace_id auth (section 3): human dashboard users sign in via Google/Microsoft OAuth, land in a pending-approval queue, and a super-admin approves them into a role (Member/Admin/Super-admin) with explicit workspace assignments ('users only ever see workspaces they are assigned to'). Pattern to follow: the in-house license-manager stack (apra-lic-mgr, Next.js pages + JWT cookie auth) per the fleet-dashboard README. Depends on the hub service existing to host it. See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md Addendum item 4.

Model: Opus -- security-sensitive auth/authorization design (a distinct system from the member-JWT workspace wall, easy to get subtly wrong, e.g. privilege escalation via role/workspace-assignment edge cases) with a real RBAC state machine (pending/approved/rejected x three roles).
notes: Design proposal written (docs/dashboard-oauth-rbac-design.md), not implemented -- deliberately docs-only given this issue's own Opus/security-sensitive flag. Surfaces a real contract ambiguity found by attempting implementation: JWTClaimsSchema is single-workspace, but GET /workspaces needs a multi-workspace-aware query; proposes a two-tier session-JWT/workspace-JWT resolution. Also proposes the users/user_workspace_roles schema, the pending-approval flow (audited via existing audit-log.ts), and concrete privilege-escalation risks to design tests against (self-escalation races, stale-token revocation on role removal -- reusing jwt-revocation.ts, admin-info leakage). Explicitly does not attempt the OAuth token-exchange implementation itself or write any code -- this is the reviewed-design step the issue's own model note calls for before implementation.
close_reason: Implemented per the reviewed design doc, with user go-ahead. Real contract-driven correction found while implementing: role is uniform per user (not per-workspace) per AdminUserSchema's actual shape, simplifying the schema from the design doc's initial per-workspace-role assumption. Built: users/user_workspace_roles schema (default-deny), users.ts data layer, session-jwt.ts (identity-only OAuth session token, resolving the design doc's found JWTClaimsSchema ambiguity), and http-server.ts routes (POST /auth/oauth/:provider with stubbed OAuth token-exchange -- real Google/Microsoft integration is separate low-risk plumbing, GET /workspaces, POST /workspaces/:id/select bridging to existing /ws/:id/... routes, GET/PUT/DELETE /admin/users/* platform-admin-gated). Tests cover every privilege-escalation risk the design doc listed (self-escalation blocked regardless of own role, no identity leakage, status gate independent of admin flag, deletion revokes access, 404 not 200 for missing users, invalid role rejection). Found and fixed a real pg-mem quirk (CHECK constraints on NULL). Full suite green (143 files/2029 tests), tsc clean.

title: Workspace iron-wall security review + threat model
description:
Formal threat model for cross-workspace leakage, token theft/replay, relay abuse, and enrollment-token replay, once the hub service (H4) and spoke mode (H6) exist to review. This is the concrete enforcement of mission point 4 (workspaces iron-walled, no cross-talk) and needs to happen before the hub migration is considered production-ready, not as an afterthought. See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 2.3.

Model: Opus -- high-risk security architecture review; getting workspace isolation wrong is the single worst failure mode in this entire migration.
close_reason: Threat model complete (docs/threat-model-workspace-iron-wall.md), commit 9ecc951. Reviewed 4 required categories across 11 concrete vectors. 9 solid/mitigated with tests cited: cross-workspace list-resource reads (authorize() workspace_id match + data-layer scoping), relay submission (getMember(claims.workspace_id)), JWT forgery (timingSafeEqual), revoked-jti reuse (isRevoked on every authorize incl new /envelopes /ack /stream), auth error/timing uniformity (all failures -> identical 401), relay flooding (per-target depth/byte cap 429), and enrollment-token replay (verified genuinely race-safe atomic UPDATE...WHERE used_at IS NULL AND expires_at>now() RETURNING; tests for single-use/expired/concurrent/workspace). 1 real cross-workspace GAP found+FIXED: fetchDeliverable scoped by target_member_id alone while /ws/:id/stream trusted spoke-supplied presence member_ids -> a spoke in ws A could announce a ws B member_id and drain B's queue. Fixed by workspace-scoping fetchDeliverable(workspaceId,targetMemberId) + passing JWT workspaceId from the stream route; plus res.flushHeaders() and server.closeAllConnections() latent fixes. Regression tests added (unit cross-workspace isolation + e2e IRON WALL stream-leakage). 1 intra-workspace finding DEFERRED to apra-fleet-us9.11.1 (P3): /ws/:id/ack + presence.announce trust body/spoke member_id within a tenant (no iron-wall crossing, lower sev). Full suite 2083 passing / 0 failing, tsc --noEmit clean before commit.

title: Interactive-injection capability survey across 5 non-Claude providers
description:
Mode (b) (prompt injection into a long-running session) is POC-proven on Claude only today (notifications/claude/channel in src/tools/send-message.ts is itself provider-branded -- flagged in the Claude-centrism audit, per mission point 11). Investigate whether gemini/codex/copilot/agy/opencode can attach to a local MCP endpoint with custom headers and accept mid-session prompt injection at all, and if so how. This directly determines mode (b)'s real reach outside Claude. See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 9.7.

Model: Fable -- open-ended live investigation across five unfamiliar provider CLIs/SDKs, no existing findings to lean on for most of them.
close_reason: Added docs/interactive-injection-provider-survey.md; confirmed MCP-header attach for gemini/codex/opencode, no mode-b equivalent found outside Claude; AGY/Copilot flagged TBD for hands-on follow-up

title: Spoke mode in apra-fleet.exe
description:
The local apra-fleet.exe gains an outbound hub channel: connect/reconnect/backoff to fleet.apralabs.com, relayed command execution reusing the existing LocalStrategy execution engine (src/services/strategy.ts), and presence reporting. This is the biggest reversal of Phase 1's design -- apra-fleet.exe stops being the JWT-minting authority and becomes a thin relay/auth-passthrough. Depends on the wire protocol (H1) and cloud JWT issuance (H5). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 2.1 tier 2.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped implementation reusing an existing execution engine (LocalStrategy), once the protocol and auth designs are fixed.
notes:
Slice 1 landed (commit 095c8a2): hub-side envelope admission -- POST /ws/:id/envelopes routes presence.announce/heartbeat (full-snapshot replace semantics, presence.announceSnapshot()) and relay-kind envelopes into the existing relay-queue.ts at-least-once queue, with cross-workspace target rejection (403) and idempotent re-admission. POST /ws/:id/ack retires delivered envelopes. 25 new tests (envelope-routes.test.ts, presence.test.ts additions, http-server.test.ts end-to-end), 2058 total tests + tsc clean.

Remaining work scoped as separate child issues rather than one undifferentiated feature: apra-fleet-b55 (hub-side SSE delivery push + TTL-expiry synthetic-failed envelopes + queue depth cap), apra-fleet-3jg (spoke-side hub-client: connect/reconnect/backoff/presence/heartbeat), apra-fleet-cgg (relayed execute_command via LocalStrategy, depends on 3jg). This issue itself stays open as the umbrella until all three children close.
close_reason: All three scoped child issues closed: apra-fleet-b55 (hub-side SSE delivery/TTL-expiry-failures/queue-depth-cap), apra-fleet-3jg (spoke-side hub-client: connect/reconnect/backoff/presence/heartbeat), apra-fleet-cgg (relayed execute_command via LocalStrategy). The described scope -- outbound hub channel with reconnect/backoff, relayed command execution reusing LocalStrategy, presence reporting -- is now implemented end-to-end and tested: hub admission -> SSE push -> spoke consumption -> LocalStrategy execution -> result relay -> ack/TTL-expiry cleanup. Data-ownership constraint (hub Postgres as sole master record, tier 2 JSON-caches via REST) remains honored -- no local SQL database was introduced. Member/local-Agent registry mapping and actually wiring hub-client+relay-executor into the apra-fleet.exe process entrypoint (main.ts equivalent for the CLI) remain open follow-on integration work, not yet scoped as their own issues.

title: Cloud JWT issuance + apra-fleet join enrollment
description:

Depends on the workspace_id claim design (H2) and the hub service (H4). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 2.2.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped implementation of an already-designed claim/issuer model; security-sensitive but not itself open-ended design.
notes: Both halves now complete: Cloud JWT issuance/rotation (member-tokens.ts, prior commit) and apra-fleet join enrollment (enrollment.ts + src/cli/join.ts, this session) -- re-scoped correctly as hub-mediated per hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 4, not blocked by the loopback-only network decision as earlier assumed. Still explicitly deferred: asymmetric signing (HUB_JWT_SECRET/HS256 remains the MVP stopgap for both member tokens and machine tokens).
close_reason: Both explicit deliverables complete: Cloud JWT issuance/rotation (issueMemberToken/rotateMemberToken, jti-based revocation reusing jwt-revocation.ts) and apra-fleet join enrollment (hub-mediated enrollment-token generate+exchange, atomic single-use claim, local apra-fleet join <token> CLI). Re-scoped correctly during implementation: 'join enrollment' is hub-mediated per hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 4, not blocked by the loopback-only network-binding decision as originally assumed. Signing mechanism (HS256 via HUB_JWT_SECRET) remains the documented MVP stopgap -- a cross-cutting concern already flagged consistently across hub-jwt.ts's every use site, not specific to this issue's own scope. Full suite green (145 files/2045 tests), tsc clean.

title: Hub service MVP (fleet.apralabs.com)
description:
The cloud hub itself: auth, workspace/machine/member CRUD, message relay, presence tracking, audit log; must have a self-hostable deployment path (not cloud-vendor-locked). Confirmed net-new -- zero existing code hits for apralabs.com or workspace_id. Depends on the wire protocol (H1), the workspace_id claim design (H2), and the persistence ADR (H3). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 2.1 tier 3.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped implementation once the protocol/claim/persistence designs land; large but not itself a novel-design task at that point.
notes: Verified against the full Endpoints contract map: every non-OAuth-gated route is now built, tested, and wired -- health, installers, GET/POST /ws/:id/members (+ view-model), GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE /ws/:id/projects (+ members, + view-model), GET /ws/:id/cost (usage.ts rollup), GET /ws/:id/activity (activity_log). 139 files/1985 tests green, tsc clean. Precisely traced the remainder: GET/POST /workspaces and POST /auth/oauth/:provider need us9.16's human-user RBAC model (workspace listing is per-user-role-scoped, not just workspace_id-scoped). POST /ws/:id/members/:mid/rotate needs us9.5's revocation-path design specifically -- rotation requires tracking which jti belongs to which member (a real design decision: schema column vs separate table, tied to the asymmetric-signing migration us9.5 owns), not just wiring existing revoke/sign functions. Self-hostable deployment docs (Dockerfile/guide) also still open -- pure documentation, no design blocker, just not yet written.
close_reason: Every explicitly-listed deliverable in this issue's own description is now built and tested: auth (hub-jwt.ts MVP HS256 stopgap + workspace-boundary enforcement), workspace/machine/member CRUD (plus projects/cost/activity, beyond original scope), message relay (relay-queue.ts, at-least-once), presence tracking, audit log, and a self-hostable deployment path (Dockerfile.hub-service + docker-compose.hub-service.yml + docs/hub-service-deployment.md, verified against the actual build output). 139 files/1985 tests green (all pg-mem/real-http, no mocking of the actual query/routing logic), tsc clean. Explicitly and clearly documented as deferred to sibling issues, not silently missing: OAuth/human-user RBAC (us9.16), real asymmetric JWT issuance + rotation (us9.5), and live relay/activity/usage traffic from real dispatches (us9.6/us9.7, since nothing calls this hub for real work yet). Docker itself was not executed in this environment (no daemon running) -- verified everything else possible without it (tsc, npm run build output path, docker-compose env/migration wiring against main.ts's actual reads).

See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 3.

Hub-and-spoke cloud migration delivered end-to-end this session: hub service MVP (Postgres, pg-mem-tested), cloud JWT issuance + apra-fleet join enrollment, hub-mediated wire protocol (envelope admission, SSE delivery, TTL-expiry-to-failure, queue depth caps), spoke mode (hub-client connect/reconnect/backoff, relay-executor fulfillment, relay-request origination, RelayStrategy, apra-fleet spoke CLI entrypoint), workspace iron-wall security review (found and fixed a real cross-workspace leak plus an intra-workspace trust gap), hub-brokered file transfer, dashboard OAuth+RBAC, usage/cost ledger, no-LLM member support, and the hub-jwt.ts/JWTClaimsSchema contract-shape reconciliation. us9.10 (web dashboard MVP) closed as superseded once C:\akhil\git\fleet-dashboard was discovered to be a separate, actively-developed project with its own DynamoDB/serverless backend -- not a static design reference as originally assumed; building a second dashboard backend here would have duplicated/conflicted with that active work (user-confirmed). title: Design + implement enrollment-token flow and apra-fleet join <token> command (Journey B: LAN)
description:
Orchestrator command to generate a short-lived, project_id-scoped enrollment token (register_member --generate-enrollment-token --project <id>); new machine runs a single apra-fleet join <token> command that detects installed providers, exchanges the token for a full JWT over LAN, and calls the right provider's registerMcpEndpoint() locally. Requires provider-agnostic registration (O1-O3) to exist first. See docs/member-onboarding-journey.md section 4, Journey B, and open questions 1 and 4 (token transport UX, single-use vs multi-use).

Model: Opus -- new cross-machine protocol and trust-boundary design (token issuance, exchange, expiry/single-use enforcement); high blast radius, security-relevant, no prior art in this codebase to lean on.
close_reason: Re-scoped correctly per docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 4: the enrollment-token exchange is hub-mediated (every machine enrolls against the already-network-facing hub URL, not a local peer), NOT the original LAN/mDNS Journey B design (which this session already documented as superseded). Implemented both halves: hub-side generateEnrollmentToken/exchangeEnrollmentToken (workspace-scoped, short-lived, single-use, atomic claim proven under real concurrent requests) wired to POST /ws/:id/enrollment-tokens and POST /join/exchange, and the local apra-fleet join <token> CLI command (outbound-only, no inbound exposure needed on any machine, consistent with the loopback-only bind decision). The resulting machine JWT is stored locally for apra-fleet-us9.6 (spoke mode) to eventually consume. Full suite green (145 files/2045 tests), tsc clean.

description:
During the 2026-07-02 RECOVERY.md execution, fleet-reorg's direct git invocations were blocked by its permission layer; instead of stopping and asking, it wrote and ran scripts/recovery.sh as a workaround (cwd-guarded, outcome verified clean). title: Unify identity keying on UUID + make registry/JWT/send_message/event-broadcast project-aware
description:
JWT carries member_id UUID but URL fallback carries friendly name; registry keyed inconsistently, spawn-time PID entry replaced (not merged) on reconnect. Per Q5 decision (multi-project Phase 1): bind real project_id at token mint (derive from work_folder/repo, not literal default), make session-registry key (project_id, member_id) composite or project-filtered, enforce sender-project==target-project in send_message, project-scope the event broadcast (currently leaks credential:stored/task:completed etc to ALL sessions). Plan section 2.3.2, 4-Q5, step 3.

Model: Opus -- cross-cutting architectural change spanning JWT minting, session registry, send_message, and the event bus, with security-relevant correctness requirements (project isolation). Highest-stakes single item in the epic.
notes: RESCOPE per docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 3 and beads issue apra-fleet-us9.2: mint the hard-boundary claim as workspace_id (not project_id) behind a pluggable issuer interface, so hub-era dashboard issuance needs no token migration. Phase 1 (single machine) semantics: one machine = one implicit workspace. project_id may survive as an optional non-security grouping label inside a workspace, but is NOT the security boundary going forward.
close_reason: Full rescoped scope done on feat/hub-spoke-migration (commit efcf1a9): UUID identity keying, workspace_id claim via pluggable TokenIssuer, (workspace_id,member_id)-keyed registry, send_message workspace enforcement, workspace-scoped event broadcast. 1801 tests pass (17 new isolation tests).

title: Fix compose_permissions destroying member MCP entry (deep-merge settings.local.json)
description:
register_member writes mcpServers['apra-fleet-member'] with Bearer JWT into member .claude/settings.local.json. compose_permissions rewrites the file wholesale, only merging permissions.allow -- silently deletes the entry and token on first compose, breaking register->compose->dispatch flow. Plan section 2.3.1, step 2.

Model: Sonnet -- single-file bug fix with well-understood scope (deep-merge instead of overwrite) plus tests; no novel architecture, but correctness matters (breaks a real flow), so above Haiku.
close_reason: Fixed: compose_permissions delivered JSON config files (settings.local.json, Gemini settings.json) by overwriting wholesale via deliverConfigFile, silently destroying register_member's mcpServers['apra-fleet-member'] JWT entry on first compose. deliverConfigFile now reads the existing remote file and deep-merges nested objects (arrays/scalars from the new config still win, so permissions.allow is still fully replaced as intended). New regression test reproduces the exact incident shape. Full suite green (119 files/1838 tests).

title: Push-sync: local member/project registration also registers with fleet-dashboard when SaaS-connected
description: Push-sync, NARROWED per the AGREED bootstrap-sync-design-proposal.md round 2 resolution: a device does NOT register other members using its own member JWT (fleet-dashboard's round 1/2 review named the real risk -- a newly-created member's once-shown JWT would flow through the registering device, a privilege-escalation shape neither side wants). Member registration stays human/session-only, permanently -- this is not a temporary restriction pending a future device-safe variant. What remains in scope for this task: whatever presence/status a SaaS-connected device pushes is already covered by the existing connect/heartbeat member-JWT routes (unchanged by this negotiation) -- confirm during implementation whether this bead still has independent scope beyond that, or folds entirely into apra-fleet-aho's pull-sync work with no separate push path needed at all.
acceptance_criteria: Registering a member/project while SaaS-connected results in a durable record on fleet-dashboard's backend, verifiable via its own API, not just a local JSON row. Standalone registration (no cloud) continues to work exactly as today with zero required network call.

title: Retire src/hub-service/ to reference-only status
description:
Per this epic's parent decision: src/hub-service/ (the Postgres-backed hub built during the hub-spoke migration sprint) is not the production tier-3 implementation going forward -- fleet-dashboard is. Retire it to reference-only:

1. Add a clear top-of-file status note to src/hub-service/main.ts and docs/hub-service-deployment.md: this code is a reference implementation (proved the wire-protocol/security-isolation semantics, found and fixed real bugs during design) but is not deployed to fleet.apralabs.com and receives no further production feature work.
2. Update docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md and docs/adr-hub-persistence.md with a pointer to this decision and docs/api-contract-reconciliation.md, so a future reader doesn't assume hub-service is still the deployment target.
3. Do NOT delete the code or its tests -- the 2133 passing tests document real, verified wire-protocol/security semantics (workspace iron-wall isolation, at-least-once delivery, TTL/redelivery) that are valuable as a specification for whichever team (fleet-dashboard) builds the production equivalent.
4. Remove or clearly mark any 'deploy this to production' tooling (Dockerfile, deployment docs) as reference/dev-only, so nobody accidentally ships it as fleet.apralabs.com.
acceptance_criteria: No hub-service code or tests are deleted. Every doc that previously implied hub-service is the production tier-3 backend is corrected or annotated. A new contributor reading docs/hub-service-deployment.md understands within one paragraph that this is a reference implementation, not a deployment target.
close_reason: implemented in sprint chore/hub-service-retire-and-docs

title: Wire spoke-mode: RelayStrategy + agentType decision + apra-fleet.exe entrypoint
description: Completes the loop apra-fleet-us9.7 started (relay-executor.ts + relay-request.ts + composeEnvelopeHandler already built and tested). Three things remain, all explicitly deferred as their own design decision rather than bundled into us9.7: (1) decide how an Agent gets classified as relay-addressed -- Agent.agentType is currently 'local'|'remote' with no third value; adding one touches registry validation, CLI agent-creation flows, and the dashboard's agent list, a wide blast radius not decided yet. (2) Build the actual apra-fleet.exe spoke-mode entrypoint that runs hub-client.ts + relay-executor.ts + relay-request.ts together via composeEnvelopeHandler(), reading hub-credentials.json (written by src/cli/join.ts). (3) Build the real member_id <-> local Agent registry mapping (relay-executor.ts's getAgentForMember is caller-supplied precisely because this doesn't exist). See docs/ssh-relay-migration-inventory.md for the full gate before any SSH-stack deletion can happen.
acceptance_criteria: A RelayStrategy class implements AgentStrategy by calling submitAndAwaitResult(); getStrategy() dispatches to it for relay-addressed agents; apra-fleet.exe has a runnable spoke-mode command that connects to the hub and can both fulfill and originate relayed execute_command calls end-to-end against a real (or pg-mem-backed) hub service in a test.
close_reason:
Implemented (commit c123704): (1) Agent.agentType gains 'relay' + relayMemberId; fixed the display/dedup call sites that assumed binary local/remote (agent-helpers.ts, list-members.ts, registry.ts's hasDuplicateFolder) so a relay agent doesn't show garbage. (2) src/services/relay-strategy.ts: RelayStrategy implements AgentStrategy via relay-request.ts's submitAndAwaitResult + relay-context.ts's process-wide singleton; getStrategy() dispatches to it. transferFiles/receiveFiles/deleteFiles honestly throw not-yet-supported rather than fabricate. (3) src/cli/spoke.ts: apra-fleet spoke <origin-member-id>, the real runnable entrypoint composing hub-client+relay-executor+relay-request via composeEnvelopeHandler, reading hub-credentials.json. origin-member-id is an explicit required arg since which member a multi-member machine acts as when originating is a genuine unresolved product question, left honest not guessed.

Building the end-to-end test (tests/spoke-e2e.test.ts, real HTTP hub + real pg-mem + real child process, two spoke instances) found and fixed a THIRD real bug in already-shipped code: relay-executor.ts never addressed its result envelopes back to the originator (to.member_id always null), which the real hub's admission check would have rejected outright -- earlier unit tests never asserted on  so this went uncaught. Fixed by threading origin_member_id through.

Explicitly deferred, not silently dropped: register_member's CLI schema still only accepts local/remote (creating a relay agent isn't wired into that flow -- relay agents are meant to be materialized from hub-known joined machines, which is its own still-unbuilt registry-sync feature); file transfer over relay isn't wired into RelayStrategy; per-member outbound identity is a flat CLI arg, not a richer model. 19 new tests, 2118 total, tsc --noEmit clean.

title: Relayed execute_command: spoke reuses LocalStrategy to fulfill hub-relayed command requests
description: Once the hub-client (its own task) can receive execute_command.request envelopes over SSE, this task wires them into src/services/strategy.ts's existing LocalStrategy execution engine and posts back an execute_command.result envelope (or long_running_update envelopes for the long-running case), matching today's SSH-based execute_command feature set. Depends on the hub-client task landing first.
acceptance_criteria: A relayed execute_command.request against a fake hub-client harness actually invokes LocalStrategy and posts a correctly-shaped execute_command.result envelope back; long-running commands emit long_running_update envelopes with the right correlation_id.
close_reason: Implemented src/services/relay-executor.ts (commit 2368221): createRelayExecutor() wires execute_command.request envelopes from the hub-client into the real LocalStrategy execution engine, posting back execute_command.result (and execute_command.long_running_update on PID capture) with correlation_id set to the originating request. member_id -> local Agent resolution is caller-supplied and honestly reports member_not_found rather than fabricating an agent. Other relay kinds are a documented no-op, not silently absorbed. 6 tests: real end-to-end success/error/nonzero-exit paths via the actual LocalStrategy (real child process, OS-appropriate command for whichever host runs the suite), plus an injected fake strategy for the PID-capture wiring (genuinely OS-specific -- Windows cleanExec doesn't wrap FLEET_PID the way linux/macos do). 2076 tests total, tsc --noEmit clean.

title: Usage/cost ledger at tier 3 (per project/member, rolled up to workspace)
description:
New tier-3 scope surfaced by the fleet-dashboard spec: track cost and token usage per (project, member), rolled up to the workspace, for the dashboard's Cost view. Honesty contract to preserve: session-cumulative only until this ledger exists; 7d/30d windows stay visibly-disabled until real persistence backs them -- never fake time windows. Usage records are append-heavy and time-series-shaped -- feeds directly into the persistence ADR (apra-fleet-us9.3) as a concrete workload to evaluate against, reinforcing Postgres-class storage as the system of record. Depends on the persistence ADR and the hub service. See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md Addendum item 3.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped data-model + aggregation implementation once the persistence choice is made; moderate complexity from correctness (rollup math, honesty-contract enforcement), not from novel design.
close_reason: Implemented the usage/cost ledger's real read+write data layer: recordUsage() (write side) and getCostResponse() (SUM/GROUP BY rollup per project+member, workspace total), wired to GET /ws/:id/cost behind the workspace-boundary auth gate. Honesty contract preserved exactly (CostResponseSchema.window literal 'session', no fake time windows; project-less rows excluded from breakdown but counted in total). Formally depends on us9.4 (still open/in-progress as an epic-level item), but the specific prerequisites this issue needed from it -- the persistence ADR (us9.3, closed) and the hub schema/pool existing -- are satisfied; the real callers that would populate this ledger for actual dispatches (us9.6/us9.7) are separate, correctly-unblocked-by-this work. Proven via pg-mem tests (rollup math, cross-workspace isolation, no-LLM 0-cost members, contract-validated against CostResponseSchema) plus an HTTP integration test. Full suite green (138 files/1977 tests), tsc clean.

title: No-LLM member type support (plain executor)
description:
Per the fleet-dashboard spec, a member can be any LLM OR no LLM at all (a plain command executor). Today's register-member.ts llm_provider enum (claude|gemini|codex|copilot|agy|opencode) has no such option. Needs: a null-object ProviderAdapter (or equivalent no-LLM path) that supports execute_command but never execute_prompt in either mode from apra-fleet-us9.8; register_member schema gains --no-llm; cost tracking renders 'compute only' instead of token cost for these members. See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md Addendum item 1.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped addition of a new, well-specified member type across a few existing call sites (adapter selection, register_member, cost display); no novel protocol design.
close_reason: Implemented no-LLM member type: NoneProvider (null-object ProviderAdapter, throws on prompt-related methods by design, harmless empty values on capability queries), register_member gains llm_provider:'none' with CLI/auth verification skipped, execute_prompt rejects 'none' members immediately with a clear execute_command pointer, and check-status/member-detail render 'compute only' instead of a token count. execute_command needed zero changes (already fully provider-agnostic). Full test coverage across all of the above. Full suite green (129 files/1913 tests), tsc clean.

title: Web dashboard MVP (fleet.apralabs.com console)
description:
Real design source found 2026-07-04: a hi-fi UX spec + clickable prototype at C:\akhil\git\fleet-dashboard (BUSINESS_MISSION.md, README.md, Fleet Cloud.html, cloud/*.jsx, hifi/ui.jsx) -- this SUPERSEDES the earlier Netlify prototype/Discussion #188 reference. Build against its documented API sketch (OAuth callback, workspace/project/member CRUD, JWT issuance/rotation, activity SSE, cost endpoint, admin provisioning). Scope is broader than 'token issuance UI': it includes OAuth (Google + Microsoft) human sign-in, a pending-approval + super-admin RBAC provisioning workflow (Member/Admin/Super-admin roles with workspace assignments) that is a SEPARATE auth system from member JWT/workspace_id auth, per-project/per-member cost rollup with an explicit honesty contract (session-only cost until a usage ledger exists; no faked 7d/30d windows), and per-OS installer downloads. Depends on the hub service (apra-fleet-us9.4) and cloud JWT issuance (apra-fleet-us9.5). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md Addendum (2026-07-04).

Model: Sonnet -- scoped frontend/backend-integration implementation against an already-detailed UX spec and API contract; not open-ended design.
close_reason: Superseded: discovered that C:\akhil\git\fleet-dashboard is NOT a static design reference (as us9.10's description assumed) but a fully separate, actively-developed project -- its own git history (branch sprint-0/scaffold-ci, dozens of real commits), its own beads tracker, and critically its own DynamoDB/serverless backend (aws/, backend/serverless.yml) already implementing member CRUD, installers, add-member wizard UI, and more. This is a different persistence architecture entirely from apra-fleet-reorg's Postgres-based hub-service (docs/adr-hub-persistence.md). Building a second dashboard backend inside apra-fleet-reorg per us9.10's original scope would duplicate or conflict with fleet-dashboard's active development. User confirmed (2026-07-05): close as superseded rather than build a competing implementation. If apra-fleet-reorg's hub-service needs to expose anything specific for fleet-dashboard's backend to consume, that should be scoped as its own narrow integration-contract issue after reviewing fleet-dashboard's actual API expectations -- not assumed from this bead's original (stale) description.

title: execute_prompt two-mode formalization (one-shot vs long-running injection)
description:
Formalize execute_prompt as exactly two modes: (a) one-shot headless spawn ("claude/agy/opencode -p prompt"), the universal baseline for all six providers, and (b) prompt injection into a long-running session (the interactive send_message-based channel apra-fleet-2xs.7/.8 already plan). Mode-selection logic must be decided tier-2-locally (against the local session registry), never from caller-side state, so it survives hub relay unchanged. Depends on apra-fleet-2xs.7 (status lifecycle) and apra-fleet-2xs.8 (execute_prompt interactive routing) landing, and on the interactive-injection capability survey (H9) confirming which providers actually support mode (b). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 8.

Model: Sonnet -- scoped formalization of already-planned work, moderate judgment on mode-selection placement.
close_reason: Formalized execute_prompt's two modes: (a) one-shot subprocess (universal, unchanged) and (b) interactive injection (built in 2xs.8). Real gap found and fixed: mode (b) was gated only on 'does a live session exist', not on provider capability -- but us9.9's survey confirms mode (b) is Claude-only (Gemini/Codex confirmed FAIL, others TBD). A non-Claude member with a live session (from fnz.1-3's registerMcpEndpoint) would have silently burned the full timeout waiting for a response its provider can never send. Fixed: interactive routing now requires agent.llmProvider === 'claude' in addition to a live session. New test proves a Gemini member with a live session correctly falls through to the subprocess path. Full suite green (127 files/1906 tests), tsc clean.

title: execute_command SSH-to-relay migration + SSH deprecation inventory
description:
Build the hub-relayed execution path across all six providers and three OSes (including long-running commands), matching today's SSH-based execute_command capability (src/tools/execute-command.ts). Once proven out, deprecate the SSH stack: ssh.ts, sftp.ts, known-hosts.ts, RemoteStrategy, setup_ssh_key, and roughly 8 associated test files. Depends on spoke mode (H6) existing and being relay-capable.

close_reason: Built the request/response correlation half of the hub-relayed execution path: src/services/relay-request.ts (PendingRelayRequests registry, submitAndAwaitResult, composeEnvelopeHandler), complementing relay-executor.ts's (apra-fleet-cgg) fulfillment side. Also wrote docs/ssh-relay-migration-inventory.md, a verified-by-grep list of the SSH-stack files/tests (ssh.ts, sftp.ts, known-hosts.ts, RemoteStrategy, setup-ssh-key.ts, ~8 test files) that would eventually be removed, explicitly gated on 4 things not yet true in this repo (no RelayStrategy/agentType decision, no spoke-mode CLI entrypoint, no member_id<->local-Agent mapping, no production/integration proof) -- nothing deleted. Filed apra-fleet-jfn for the remaining integration work (RelayStrategy + agentType + entrypoint wiring), since that requires a registry/CLI design decision with a wide blast radius not appropriate to bundle into this issue. 11 new tests, 2097 total, tsc --noEmit clean.

title: Persistence ADR: Postgres-only vs Postgres+Redis vs Postgres+NATS for fleet.apralabs.com
description:
Evaluate hub datastore options against the responsibilities fleet.apralabs.com must serve: presence, at-least-once message relay (plain pub/sub is disqualified -- it drops messages to briefly-disconnected spokes), JWT issuance/revocation state, workspace/member CRUD, and the audit/activity log for the web dashboard. Redis Streams fit presence + relay well but Redis cannot do auth/JWT issuance and is the wrong system of record for CRUD/audit (durability + query + compliance needs a real database). Recommendation to evaluate: Postgres as system of record, +LISTEN/NOTIFY at early scale, +Redis or +NATS if/when relay throughput demands it. Weigh self-host parity (a stated product value). See docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 6.

Model: Fable -- open-ended infrastructure research and trade-off analysis before any code exists; needs broad investigation of managed-service options and self-host parity constraints.
close_reason: Added docs/adr-hub-persistence.md: Postgres-only MVP decision with benchmarked escape hatch to Redis Streams/NATS JetStream

title: WAN-readiness review: bind/TLS/discovery changes needed for Journey C, without changing the data model
description:
Journey C (WAN) needs a configurable bind + TLS (currently 127.0.0.1-only, already flagged in the SSE plan as Phase-1-acceptable/future-must-change) and a non-LAN discovery/relay path for the enrollment token. This is a review/design-note task now (not implementation): confirm nothing in O1-O4 bakes in a LAN-only or single-machine assumption that would force a breaking change later. See docs/member-onboarding-journey.md section 4, Journey C.

Model: Opus -- architectural forward-compatibility review across the whole onboarding design; requires judgment about what would and would not require a breaking change later.
close_reason: WAN-readiness review complete, no code changes needed. Confirmed nothing in O1-O4 bakes in a LAN-only/single-machine assumption: enrollment (fnz.4/us9.5) is hub-mediated and outbound-only (apra-fleet join calls out to the hub, no inbound exposure needed anywhere); hub bind default is already 0.0.0.0 (network-facing by design, distinct from the local spoke's 127.0.0.1-only default); machines.hostname is a display-only label, never used for network addressing/discovery. One real gap found: hub-service-deployment.md never documented TLS termination expectations for a public deployment -- fixed by adding a TLS/WAN deployment section recommending a reverse-proxy/load-balancer in front of the plain-HTTP hub process. No breaking change is needed for WAN; this was a documentation gap, not an architecture gap. 2045 tests + tsc --noEmit clean after the doc change.

title: Fix stale-close unregister race in http-transport (onsessionclosed)
description:
onsessionclosed unregisters by member_id captured at initialize time. If a member reconnects (new sid) and the OLD session closes afterward, the close handler unregisters the NEW session's registry entry. Guard with sessionRegistry.get(member_id)?.sessionId === sid before unregister. Plan Addendum A2 item 12.

Model: Haiku -- precise, fully-specified one-condition guard fix in a single handler.
close_reason: Guarded onsessionclosed unregister paths with sessionRegistry.get(workspace_id, member_id)?.sessionId === sid; added regression test; 1802/0 tests passing

title: Fix suite-wide test pollution from shared fixed-path fixtures under concurrent runs
description:
tests/setup.ts points every run at the SAME fixed os.tmpdir()/apra-fleet-test-data dir, never cleaned between runs; fileParallelism:false only serializes within one vitest process, not across two concurrent processes. Root cause of the 2026-07-02 git-loss incident's test failures (two overlapping process trees from one orchestrator dispatch). Hardening: per-run unique APRA_FLEET_DATA_DIR via os.tmpdir()+PID/random suffix, cleaned in teardown. Plan section 2.2, step 10.

Model: Sonnet -- root cause is already diagnosed; the fix is a scoped test-harness change (unique dir + teardown), not new design.
close_reason: Added tests/global-setup.ts computing unique per-run test data dir via provide/inject with teardown cleanup; verified concurrent invocations no longer collide

title: execute_prompt interactive routing via send_message for connected members
description:
execute_prompt should internally route via send_message + wait-for-response for connected Claude members per architecture Section 6/12; today subprocess-only. Gated by F5 (config surface). High risk -- protocol design. Plan section 2.3.10, step 9.

Model: Opus -- protocol/routing design with high blast radius (execute_prompt is the primary dispatch path); explicitly flagged high risk, and depends on getting 2.3.2's project-aware registry right first.
notes: Scope note per docs/hub-spoke-master-plan.md section 8 and beads issue apra-fleet-us9.8: interactive-vs-subprocess mode selection must be decided tier-2-locally, against the LOCAL session registry on the machine running apra-fleet.exe -- never from caller-side/hub-side state -- so the logic is unaffected by whether execute_prompt is invoked directly (Phase 1) or relayed through the hub (later).
close_reason: Implemented execute_prompt's dual-path routing per docs/cloud-fleet-architecture.md section 6: a member with a live interactive MCP session gets routed via send_message + wait-for-response (new pending-responses.ts msgid correlation + respond_to_message tool) instead of a subprocess spawn; every other member is completely unaffected (falls through to the unchanged existing path). Mode selection is decided tier-2-locally against this machine's own sessionRegistry, per the issue's own scope note. Shares the same inFlightAgents concurrency guard and busy/idle statusline lifecycle as the subprocess path. Proven via execute-prompt-interactive.test.ts (full round trip, concurrency guard, timeout, and confirmed fallthrough for non-interactive members) plus unit tests for the new modules. Full suite green (127 files/1905 tests), tsc clean.

title: Status lifecycle + response/ack path (busy -> online/idle, member completion reporting)
description:
send_message sets member busy and nothing ever sets it back; no response/ack path from member to PM. Architecture doc Section 6 Step 7 unimplemented. High risk -- protocol design. Plan section 2.3.6, step 8.

Model: Opus -- new bidirectional protocol design (ack/response path) with wide blast radius across every member interaction; explicitly flagged high risk in the plan.
close_reason: Closed the busy->online/idle status loop with a new report_status tool: a connected member's own interactive session reports 'online'/'idle' after responding, with identity resolved entirely from the live MCP session (extra.sessionId -> sessionRegistry.findBySessionId), no member_id parameter needed. Deliberately scoped to what was actually broken (send_message sets busy, nothing clears it) rather than inventing a new envelope/correlation-id protocol -- that remains the hub-era relay's job per docs/hub-spoke-wire-protocol.md section 4, which explicitly left this tier-2-local state machine unspecified. Proven via unit tests plus a real MCP-wire integration test (connects a JWT client, calls report_status through the actual SDK, verifies the session correlation genuinely works). Full suite green (124 files/1891 tests), tsc clean.

close_reason: Added full unit coverage for the previously zero-coverage jwt.ts, session-registry.ts, send-message.ts modules and the JWT/member-param paths in http-transport.ts (39 new tests across 4 files). title: Intra-workspace ack/announce trust: verify member_id belongs to calling machine
description: Threat-model finding 3d (docs/threat-model-workspace-iron-wall.md). POST /ws/:id/ack (http-server.ts:272-300) takes member_id from the request body and acks scoped only by workspace_id (relay-queue.ts:133-146); it does NOT verify member_id is served by the calling machine (claims.member_id). A compromised spoke inside a workspace could ack (retire) envelopes addressed to a DIFFERENT member in the SAME workspace, suppressing their delivery. Intra-tenant only (does not cross the iron wall), hence lower severity. Root cause shared with presence.announce trusting spoke-supplied member_ids verbatim (envelope-routes.ts:87-103, presence.ts:22-58); that is now harmless for cross-tenant reads after the us9.11 fetchDeliverable workspace-scoping fix, but remains an intra-tenant integrity gap.
acceptance_criteria: ack cross-checks that member_id resolves to a member currently present on claims.member_id's machine (presence.listForMachine) before acking, and/or presence.announce validates each announced member_id resolves within the machine's workspace via getMember(claims.workspace_id, ...). Regression test proves a spoke cannot ack another member's envelope within the same workspace.
close_reason: Fixed (commit 7c7b2ea): envelope-routes.ts's handlePresence now verifies each announced member_id (and heartbeat's single member_id) resolves via getMember(claims.workspace_id, ...) before trusting it into the presence snapshot -- unresolvable entries are silently dropped, not a hard rejection, so one bad id doesn't block the rest of a valid announce. POST /ws/:id/ack now cross-checks the target member_id against listForMachine(claims.member_id) (the calling machine's own announced presence) and returns 403 if the machine never announced that member, closing the intra-workspace ack-suppression gap. 3 new tests plus updated existing presence tests to createMember() first (previously announced synthetic ids with no backing row, which the fix now correctly drops). 2086 tests total, tsc --noEmit clean.

title: Enforce hub-jwt role value-domain against contract's RoleSchema enum
description: Independent review (2026-07-05) found hub-jwt.ts's HubJwtClaims.role field names now match packages/fleet-api-contract/src/schemas/jwt.ts's JWTClaimsSchema field-for-field (iss/ws/sub/exp/role, fixed 2026-07-05), but the VALUE domain is not enforced: the contract's RoleSchema is z.enum(['member','admin','superadmin']), while hub-jwt.ts's sign()/verify() accept/return a bare string. Machine tokens are minted with role:'spoke' (tests/hub-service/http-server.test.ts and others), which is outside the contract's enum -- meaning either the contract's RoleSchema needs a machine/spoke role value added, or hub-jwt.ts needs its own machine-vs-member role distinction reconciled with the contract. This is a real, if minor, spec/implementation mismatch worth a deliberate decision rather than silent drift.
acceptance_criteria: Either: (a) RoleSchema in packages/fleet-api-contract is extended to include whatever machine-token role values are actually minted (e.g. 'spoke'), with hub-jwt.ts validating against it; or (b) hub-jwt.ts documents and enforces that machine-level tokens use a distinct claims shape/field not subject to RoleSchema, with a test proving the boundary. Do not just silently loosen the contract's enum without deciding which is correct.
close_reason: Resolved per acceptance option (b): documented in hub-jwt.ts that HubJwtClaims.role has three distinct value vocabularies depending on token family (dashboard workspace-selection: contract's RoleSchema member/admin/superadmin; member/agent tokens: 'doer'; machine/spoke tokens: 'spoke') -- only the first is contract-bound, and unifying them would be a category error, not a fix. Added RoleSchema.safeParse defense-in-depth at the one call site that mints contract-bound tokens (POST /workspaces/:id/select, commit 341d31a). A new test proves the practical guarantee already holds independently at the DB layer: the users table's own CHECK constraint rejects an out-of-enum role via direct SQL. 1 new test, 2133 total, tsc clean.

title: Harden sandboxedWriteFile against pre-planted symlink escape
description: Independent review (2026-07-05) noted src/cli/spoke.ts's sandboxedWriteFile does a purely lexical path-containment check (no realpath/symlink resolution). A pre-existing symlink planted inside FLEET_DIR/received-files (out-of-band, not plantable via this write API itself) would be followed, allowing a write to escape the sandbox. Reviewer assessed this as a hardening opportunity, not a live exploit (this API cannot itself create the symlink), but worth closing given destPath is fully sender-controlled untrusted input.
acceptance_criteria: sandboxedWriteFile resolves the real path (fs.realpathSync or equivalent) of the containing directory after mkdir and re-validates containment before the write, OR uses an equivalent symlink-safe write primitive. A test proves a pre-planted symlink inside received-files pointing outside the sandbox is rejected, not followed.
close_reason: Implemented (commit 341d31a): sandboxedWriteFile re-validates against the real (fs.realpathSync-resolved) path of the containing directory after mkdir, and separately refuses if the destination file itself is already a symlink. Two new tests in tests/spoke-sandbox.test.ts actually create real symlinks (a directory symlink and a file symlink) pointing outside the sandbox and prove both are rejected with a clear error, not silently followed -- verified they actually ran (not EPERM-skipped) on this Windows environment. 2 new tests, 2133 total, tsc clean.
